title: The U.S. Navy’s Subsea Rare Earth Vulnerability
gist: A defense-tech investor argues the Columbia-class submarine's propulsion, stealth, and missile guidance depend almost entirely on rare earths refined in China, and that fixing this needs a financial architecture of offtake commitments, loan guarantees, and binding multi-year procurement, not stockpiles or single-champion equity stakes.

insights.claim: The Columbia-class submarine's permanent-magnet motor, stealth acoustic systems, and submarine-launched missile guidance all depend on dysprosium, neodymium, terbium, and samarium-cobalt magnets whose refining China controls.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: A single-point supply dependency on China sits inside America's next-gen nuclear deterrent, a strategic and procurement risk.
insights.quote: What makes all of that possible — the propulsion, the stealth, the strike precision — depends almost entirely on rare earths refined in China.

insights.claim: Twelve Columbia-class boats, each carrying 16 Trident IIs, will replace the Ohio-class fleet entering service over the 2030s and 2040s, with the first patrol scheduled for 2030.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: The 2030 deadline collides with a 5-7 year build time for rare earth processing, making the timing the crux of the argument.
insights.locator: Twelve of these boats will replace the aging Ohio-class fleet

insights.claim: This subsea rare earth dependency is far less addressed than the radar and semiconductor (e.g. gallium nitride) vulnerabilities that have consumed Washington's attention.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: Flags an under-priced supply-chain risk relative to the more-discussed semiconductor exposure.
insights.quote: perhaps the Navy’s most consequential and least discussed vulnerability.
insights.locator: This is perhaps the Navy’s most consequential

themes.theme: China's structural monopoly and price weapon

insights.claim: As of 2024 China controlled 91 percent of global rare earth separation and refining and roughly 99 percent of heavy rare earth processing specifically.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: Quantifies the near-total chokepoint, especially for the heavy rare earths the defense supply chain most needs.
insights.quote: China controlled 91 percent of global rare earth separation and refining, and approximately 99 percent of heavy rare earth processing specifically.
insights.locator: The result is that as of 2024

insights.claim: Rare earth refining generates roughly 2,000 tons of toxic waste per ton of oxides and only works economically with state support, which China provided via ~$10 billion in direct subsidies between 2010 and 2019.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: Explains why unsubsidized Western producers can't match Chinese economics, the root market failure.
insights.quote: For every ton of rare earth oxides refined, the process generates approximately 2,000 tons of toxic waste.
insights.locator: It is a multi-stage chemical gauntlet

insights.claim: China uses a repeatable price weapon: let prices spike until a Western competitor commits capital, then flood the market with cheap oxides until that competitor fails, as it did to Molycorp's Mountain Pass restart, which went bankrupt in 2015.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific mechanism any Western policy fix must neutralize, and why mining alone fails.
insights.quote: letting prices rise until a Western competitor commits capital, then lowering prices until that competitor fails
insights.locator: The pattern of letting prices rise

insights.claim: Beijing's June 2026 decision to place MP Materials and USA Rare Earth on its export control list shows China is actively targeting the very companies trying to build domestic processing.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: Direct hit on the publicly traded names leading U.S. rare earth onshoring; a near-term catalyst for those equities and policy.
insights.quote: makes clear that China is actively targeting the companies trying to solve the problem.
insights.locator: Beijing’s June 2026 decision to place MP Materials

themes.theme: The capital gap and demand-signal fix

insights.claim: Musto argues the missing ingredient is not a stockpile but a financial architecture of offtake commitments, loan guarantees, and binding procurement floor prices that make domestic heavy-rare-earth processing economical to build and run.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: Distinguishes durable industrial capacity from buffer stockpiles, reframing where federal dollars should go.
insights.quote: A stockpile of processed materials buys strategic buffer time, not domestic industrial capacity.
insights.locator: These are meaningful steps, but it is worth being precise

insights.claim: The Replicator program shows a credible demand signal works: a specific, dollar-backed pledge drew more than 500 commercial companies, of which thirty won awards and capital followed.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: A template for catalyzing private capital into rare earth processing via a clear DoD buy commitment.
insights.quote: more than 500 commercial companies competed for Replicator-related contracts, thirty received awards, and capital certainly followed the demand signal.
insights.locator: But Replicator at least told the market

insights.claim: The June 2026 $500 million Office of Strategic Capital conditional loan to Phoenix Tailings, structured to catalyze another $500 million private, signals rare earths are becoming a priority.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: A concrete deal showing the loan-guarantee model the author advocates is starting to deploy.
insights.quote: $500 million from the Office of Strategic Capital, structured to catalyze an additional $500 million in private capital

insights.claim: Equity stakes in a single champion (as with the ~$11 billion Intel stake) are the wrong model because the rare earth supply chain needs viable markets for dozens of companies, not one propped-up incumbent.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: Argues against the equity-stake industrial policy gaining traction, favoring market-restoring mechanisms instead.
insights.quote: concentrating nearly $11 billion in a single incumbent doesn’t fix the underlying market structure.
insights.locator: I think that’s the wrong model

insights.claim: The Development Finance Corporation should fund allied processing in Australia, Japan, Canada, and the UK, but recent DFC rare earth commitments have flowed to Brazil and Angola instead.
insights.speaker: Ryan Musto
insights.so_what: Identifies a misallocation in allied critical-minerals financing that policy could redirect.
insights.locator: The allied dimension deserves substantial attention
